docs.title: Dmitry M. - System Analyst / Solution Design Analyst
docs.text:
I work on system analysis and solution design for enterprise and product systems.

My strongest area is turning complex business domains into implementable system models: data structures, roles, user scenarios, integration flows, API contracts, constraints, edge cases, documentation, and development-ready tasks.

I have a hands-on engineering background: years of infrastructure and internal IT systems experience, Java/Spring, SQL, PostgreSQL, integration specifications, GIS/data tooling, and applied prototypes. This allows me to work not only with requirements, but also with the technical consequences of design decisions.

docs.title: Professional Focus
docs.text:
• System analysis for enterprise and B2B systems
  • Technical solution design
  • Data models, reference data, and entity lifecycles
  • API contracts and integration flows
  • RBAC, access rights, and context-specific constraints
  • Data import, validation, processing, and traceability
  • Development-oriented documentation: C4, DFD, BPMN, ADR, specifications
  • Backend-aware analysis: design with implementation, operations, and support in mind

docs.title: Where I Bring the Most Value
docs.text:
I am useful in situations where simply “collecting requirements” is not enough.

Typical cases:

  • the domain is complex, ambiguous, or poorly formalized;
  • a business idea has to be translated into a system structure;
  • processes, data, roles, integrations, and constraints have to be connected;
  • development teams need clear models, contracts, and scenarios;
  • an existing solution has to be analyzed, stabilized, or prepared for further development;
  • there is a missing technical layer between business, analysis, architecture, and engineering.

docs.title: Engineering Foundation
docs.text:
Analysis and design: System Analysis, Solution Design, Data Modeling, Integration Design, API Design, ETL Specifications, C4, DFD, BPMN, ADR.

Backend and data: Java, Spring Boot, JDBC, PostgreSQL, SQL, Python.

Infrastructure and delivery context: Docker, Linux, Nginx, GitHub, GitLab, AWS basics, S3-compatible storage, observability basics.

AI-assisted systems: controlled LLM execution, tool registry, execution traces, evidence-backed responses. I treat AI not as a replacement for architecture and analysis, but as an additional system layer that requires boundaries, verification, and traceability.

docs.title: Botanical B2B SaaS
docs.text:
A B2B SaaS platform for botanical collection management: multi-tenancy, RBAC, scientific taxonomy, plant specimens, departments, locations, lists, Excel import, images, QR labels, and public organization pages.

My role: system design, domain modeling, data structure, backend/frontend coordination, import flows, access control, architecture decisions, and MVP implementation.

  • details

docs.title: Enterprise GIS / Railway Graph Tooling
docs.text:
An internal tool for working with railway infrastructure data: graph preparation, geodata processing, visualization, editing, and data-loading optimization.

My role: system analysis, data model design, prototyping, Java/Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, OpenLayers, and preparation of the solution for internal use.

  • details

docs.title: FMCG Decision-Support Initiatives
docs.text:
Participation in analysis and solution design for demand forecasting and production planning initiatives in an FMCG context.

My role: system analysis, process description, integration flows, DFD/C4, SQL, specifications, and collaboration with R&D, DS and architecture teams.

  • details

docs.title: AI Operational Intelligence Prototype
docs.text:
A management analytics prototype with a controlled execution loop: LangGraph-based orchestration, tool registry, execution traces, and answers grounded in verifiable data.

My role: execution flow design, scenarios, synthetic datasets, tool registry, prototyping, and validation of the approach.

  • details

docs.title: Serverless Transcriber
docs.text:
A serverless prototype of a transcription service on AWS: audio upload, file storage, processing execution, result persistence, and API access.

My role: solution architecture, backend/API design, AWS Lambda, S3, API Gateway, DynamoDB, Terraform, and Python.

  • details

docs.title: Target Roles
docs.text:
• Senior System Analyst
  • Technical System Analyst
  • Solution Design Analyst
  • System Analyst on a Solution Architecture track
  • Associate / Junior Solution Architect in a team with a strong architecture function

docs.title: Positioning
docs.text:
I do not position myself as a pure business analyst, pure backend developer, or full enterprise architect.

My working area is between business context, system models, data, integrations, constraints, and implementation. I help turn complex or unclear problems into systems that can be discussed, estimated, implemented, and evolved.

docs.title: Services & Approach
docs.text: I work as a hands-on System Analyst and Technical Designer. My primary role is to bring structure to complex or chaotic environments, providing a clear roadmap for development and deployment.

docs.title: Rescuing and Scaling AI Prototypes
docs.text: AI-assisted coding is a massive accelerator, but it often produces fragmented architecture. I help founders take their generated MVP and refactor it into a production-ready product. This includes setting up clear boundaries between the UI, APIs, databases, and external services, ensuring the code can be safely updated and deployed to a VPS or cloud environment.

docs.title: System & Architecture Design
docs.text: I translate unclear business goals into a structured system blueprint. This involves defining the domain model, mapping out user scenarios, and creating architectural artifacts (C4, Sequence Diagrams) that serve as a direct guide for engineering. I build working prototypes if it is the fastest way to validate a solution.

docs.title: Data Modeling & Access Control
docs.text: I design robust data models and integration contracts (APIs, ETL specifications). A key focus is on security and context-aware Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). Instead of relying on generic defaults, I design granular access models tailored to your business logic (for example, isolating responsibilities into specific roles like PLATFORM_ADMIN and PLATFORM_SUPPORT).

docs.title: Where I Bring the Most Value
docs.text:
• You have an AI-generated MVP that needs architectural stabilization before launch.
  • Requirements are raw, contradictory, or constantly changing.
  • You need to design robust data structures, APIs, and integrations from scratch.
  • The project requires strict traceability, auditability, and operational constraints.

docs.title: Confluence Documentation Practice Analyzer
docs.text: Type: Internal tooling / Analytics chapter initiative Role: System Analyst, Internal Tooling Developer Status: implemented ad-hoc utility

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
As part of analytics chapter activities, there was a need to better understand how analytical documentation was created and maintained in Confluence: document volume, authorship, structure, usage of agreed formatting patterns, and adherence to internal documentation practices.

The goal was not to “score people”, but to make documentation practices more visible and provide a basis for improving consistency, onboarding, and knowledge reuse across analytical teams.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Confluence contained a large number of analytical materials created by different authors and teams. Manual review was slow and inconsistent. It was difficult to answer practical questions:

  • which materials were created or updated by a specific analyst;
  • whether documents used agreed corporate macros and formatting patterns;
  • whether pages followed accepted documentation conventions;
  • where documentation practices were strong or inconsistent;
  • which examples could be reused as references for the analytics chapter.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Built an ad-hoc console utility that used the Confluence API to export pages created or updated by a specified analyst, identified by domain login, and prepared the data for further analysis.

The utility supported structured extraction and inspection of page metadata and page content, including detection of selected Confluence macros used in internal documentation standards.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
• Retrieved Confluence pages by author / domain login.
  • Extracted page metadata: title, space, URL, author, creation date, update date.
  • Parsed page storage format for selected Confluence macros.
  • Prepared output for further manual or automated analysis.
  • Supported documentation quality review within analytics chapter activities.

docs.title: Example analysis dimensions
docs.text:
• Number of pages created or updated by analyst.
  • Distribution by Confluence space or project area.
  • Presence of required or recommended macros.
  • Usage of agreed documentation patterns.
  • Pages suitable as reference examples.
  • Pages requiring cleanup or structural improvement.

docs.title: Technical approach
docs.text:
• Confluence REST API integration.
  • Authentication through corporate access mechanism.
  • Filtering by author / domain login.
  • Parsing Confluence storage-format content.
  • Export to structured files for further analysis.

docs.title: What this project demonstrates
docs.text:
This utility demonstrates a practical approach to documentation governance: using lightweight automation to make documentation practices observable, measurable, and improvable.

It also reflects my broader working style: when a process problem appears repeatedly, I prefer to formalize it, extract data, and build a small tool that turns subjective discussion into evidence-based improvement.

docs.title: FMCG Supply Chain Optimization (Demand Forecasting & Planning)
docs.text:
Industry: FMCG / Machine Learning & Advanced Analytics (2025 – 2026)

Designed the system landscape, data models, and integration contracts for a demand forecasting (ML) and production planning (Gurobi Solver) system. Acted as a System Designer, bridging the gap between business requirements and Data Architecture.

  • Demand Forecast ML Module:
  • Conducted business process modeling (AS-IS / TO-BE) and formalized business and functional requirements (BRD/FRD).
  • Conducted a Build vs. Buy analysis, providing technical justification for the in-house development scenario to ensure the required system flexibility.
  • Visualized the target solution architecture by preparing a comprehensive suite of C4 model diagrams (Context, Container, Component) and Data Flow Diagrams (DFD).

  • Designed ETL integration contracts for data preparation to feed ML models (Feature Engineering).

  • Production Planning Module (Gurobi Solver):

  • Designed the relational data model, preparing DDL scripts for the Prototype and MVP stages.
  • Developed API contracts for backend service interactions.

  • Designed ETL data contracts for seamless data transfer into the mathematical optimization solver.

  • Result: Delivered a complete package of project and integration documentation, enabling business stakeholders and Enterprise Architects to transparently evaluate the complexity of in-house development and make the final product decision.

Deeper info is under NDA.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: B2E GIS platform for visualizing reports from internal enterprise tabular systems with spatial binding to railway infrastructure and the track-segment graph.

docs.title: Role and Contribution
docs.text:
Systems Analyst. Elicited and formalized functional requirements, prepared project documentation, and translated business needs into implementation tasks for developers.

Also implemented ad-hoc technical solutions, including geospatial data processing, tile server configuration, GIS router setup, and pre-production readiness activities.

Further details are under NDA.

docs.title: Project value
docs.text: An enterprise AI prototype for evidence-based managerial analytics - an AI Executive Analyst with verifiable evidence that helps executives investigate business questions without opaque AI reasoning.

docs.title: What this demonstrates
docs.text:
This project demonstrates my ability to move from classical systems analysis into enterprise AI solution architecture.

It shows that I can take an ambiguous AI product idea and turn it into a working, constrained, and demonstrable system: define the architecture, model the data, design diagnostic workflows, build a prototype, prepare synthetic scenarios, expose controlled tools, and make LLM outputs traceable enough for enterprise discussion.

The core value is not "using an LLM". The core value is designing a system where AI reasoning is bounded by architecture, evidence, tool contracts, and auditability.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
An enterprise AI prototype for evidence-based managerial analytics.

The product direction is an AI Executive Analyst with verifiable evidence: a decision-support layer that helps executives and domain owners investigate business questions, detect operational risks, explain deviations, and prepare management actions without relying on opaque AI reasoning.

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text: Agent runtime: LangGraph, FastAPI, Python Interface: Open WebUI as temporary demo harness Data layer: PostgreSQL, Qdrant, MinIO, Redis AI integration: OpenAI-compatible LLM API, prompt-based workflow control Architecture patterns: Tool Gateway, Tool Registry, playbook-based diagnostics, RAG, semantic layer, run trace, evidence trail Development approach: AI-assisted prototyping, synthetic data generation, scenario-driven MVP validation

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
An enterprise AI prototype for evidence-based managerial analytics.

The system is designed around a simple principle: the LLM does not “answer from memory” and does not get direct access to business data. Instead, it operates inside a controlled execution loop: it selects a diagnostic playbook, calls permitted backend tools, retrieves structured metrics and document evidence, preserves the execution trace, and generates an executive-level answer grounded in verifiable data.

The product direction is an AI Executive Analyst with verifiable evidence: a decision-support layer that helps executives and domain owners investigate business questions, detect operational risks, explain deviations, and prepare management actions without relying on opaque AI reasoning.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Typical executive analytics requires manual work across BI reports, spreadsheets, task trackers, meeting notes, documents, and domain experts. LLMs can help with synthesis, but a free-form chat over corporate data is unsafe and unreliable: it can hallucinate, lose context, call the wrong data source, or produce conclusions that cannot be audited.

This project explores how to make LLM-based managerial analytics controlled, traceable, and useful for enterprise environments.

docs.title: Implemented MVP Capabilities
docs.text:
• Chat-based executive query interface through Open WebUI.
  • LangGraph/FastAPI agent-lab runtime for diagnostic workflows.
  • Tool-server layer for controlled backend tool execution.
  • Tool Registry concept for tool discovery and playbook constraints.
  • Financial Operations playbook for margin, revenue, discounts, COGS, and product mix diagnostics.
  • Executive Operations / Delivery-oriented playbook for roadmap, delivery, ITSM, PMO, meetings, tasks, and KPI anomaly analysis.
  • Synthetic enterprise dataset with connected business domains.
  • PostgreSQL-backed metric tools.
  • Qdrant/MinIO-backed document evidence direction for RAG.
  • Playbook routing based on user intent.
  • Execution timeline and run details for transparency.
  • Same-language response handling for user-facing answers.
  • Initial guardrails against wrong fallback behavior, repeated tool calls, and unsupported analysis paths.

docs.title: Role and Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Designed the overall architecture of the MVP: chat harness, agent runtime, playbook routing, tool gateway, tool registry, structured data layer, document RAG layer, and execution trace.
  • Built a LangGraph/FastAPI-based laboratory agent runtime for controlled diagnostic workflows.
  • Defined the playbook-based approach for domain-specific diagnostic workflows.
  • Designed the controlled tool access model (see Security and Access Model).
  • Implemented and evolved the Tool Registry concept (see Architecture).
  • Prepared synthetic enterprise demo data (see Domain Model).
  • Developed the evidence and transparency approach (see Integration Flows).
  • Configured Open WebUI as a temporary chat interface (see Trade-offs).
  • Shaped the product direction toward executive reports, signal cards, evidence graphs, proactive alerts, and future backend-native orchestration.

docs.title: Synthetic enterprise dataset
docs.text:
Prepared synthetic enterprise demo data for a fashion/retail/manufacturing company, including connected business domains:

  • Financial - margin, revenue, discounts, COGS, product mix
  • Delivery - roadmap and delivery metrics
  • ITSM - service and incident context
  • PMO - project and portfolio management data
  • Meetings - decisions and meeting notes
  • Documents - document evidence for RAG
  • Scenario-based anomalies - cross-domain diagnostic scenarios

docs.title: Tool Gateway pattern
docs.text: All data access goes through controlled HTTP tools with explicit input contracts, validation, structured output, and metadata.

docs.title: Playbook-based diagnostics
docs.text:
The system routes questions to domain-specific diagnostic playbooks instead of exposing all tools to the LLM at once.

Each business domain exposes a bounded set of allowed tools, diagnostic steps, constraints, and expected evidence.

docs.title: Tool Registry
docs.text: Implemented and evolved the Tool Registry concept as a machine-readable catalog of available tools, schemas, domains, constraints, and allowed playbooks.

docs.title: Evidence-first answers
docs.text: Final responses must be based on tool outputs, document evidence, calculations, or explicitly stated limitations.

docs.title: Run trace as a trust layer
docs.text: Each diagnostic run preserves the selected playbook, tool calls, parameters, outputs, and reasoning checkpoints for debugging and audit.

docs.title: Evidence and transparency approach
docs.text: Developed the evidence and transparency approach: selected playbook, tool calls, parameters, tool results, execution timeline, run details, and JSON-level debug visibility.

docs.title: Controlled LLM execution
docs.text: The LLM reasons and plans, but data access is delegated to controlled backend tools - not free chat over corporate data.

docs.title: Controlled tool access model
docs.text: Designed the controlled tool access model where the LLM never queries PostgreSQL, Qdrant, or MinIO directly.

docs.title: Backend as control plane
docs.text: The backend defines available tools, permissions, validation rules, execution boundaries, auditability, and response structure.

docs.title: Lab runtime separated from target architecture
docs.text: LangGraph and Open WebUI are used as fast MVP/lab tools. The target product architecture assumes a backend-native control plane, dedicated UI, Tool Gateway, semantic layer, report service, and audit trail.

docs.title: Open WebUI as temporary interface
docs.text:
Configured Open WebUI as a temporary chat interface for MVP demonstration rather than building a dedicated executive UI upfront.

See also Architecture Decision Records.

docs.title: Financial Performance Diagnosis
docs.text:
Example question:

Why did gross margin drop in March?

The system selects a financial diagnostic playbook, calls metric tools for gross margin, revenue, discounts, COGS, and product mix, then produces an executive summary with evidence and limitations.

docs.title: Operational / KPI Anomaly Diagnosis
docs.text:
Example question:

Why is time-to-market unstable while local team KPIs look normal?

The system selects an operational diagnostic playbook and investigates delivery, PMO, ITSM, meeting decisions, and related evidence to detect cross-functional bottlenecks that are not visible in isolated KPI dashboards.

docs.title: Cross-Domain Management Hypothesis
docs.text:
Target scenario:

Identify the top problematic projects, explain the selection criteria, describe the issue for each project, and prepare a meeting agenda for product owners.

This scenario demonstrates the intended product direction: not just retrieving delayed tasks, but turning structured and document evidence into a management-ready diagnostic brief.

docs.title: "What can you do?"
docs.text: Available playbooks and tools

docs.title: Financial playbook: gross margin drop hypothesis
docs.text: Financial performance diagnosis with tool-based evidence Financial performance diagnosis chars report Financial performance diagnosis called tools

docs.title: Executive Operations playbook: KPI anomaly
docs.text: Operational anomaly diagnosis across delivery, ITSM, PMO, and documents

docs.title: What This Demonstrates
docs.text:
This project demonstrates my ability to move from classical systems analysis into enterprise AI solution architecture.

It shows that I can take an ambiguous AI product idea and turn it into a working, constrained, and demonstrable system: define the architecture, model the data, design diagnostic workflows, build a prototype, prepare synthetic scenarios, expose controlled tools, and make LLM outputs traceable enough for enterprise discussion.

The core value is not "using an LLM". The core value is designing a system where AI reasoning is bounded by architecture, evidence, tool contracts, and auditability.

docs.text: An enterprise AI prototype for evidence-based managerial analytics - an AI Executive Analyst with verifiable evidence that helps executives investigate business questions without opaque AI reasoning.

docs.title: What this demonstrates
docs.text:
This project demonstrates my ability to move from classical systems analysis into enterprise AI solution architecture.

It shows that I can take an ambiguous AI product idea and turn it into a working, constrained, and demonstrable system: define the architecture, model the data, design diagnostic workflows, build a prototype, prepare synthetic scenarios, expose controlled tools, and make LLM outputs traceable enough for enterprise discussion.

The core value is not "using an LLM". The core value is designing a system where AI reasoning is bounded by architecture, evidence, tool contracts, and auditability.

docs.title: Implemented MVP Capabilities
docs.text:
• Chat-based executive query interface through Open WebUI.
  • LangGraph/FastAPI agent-lab runtime for diagnostic workflows.
  • Tool-server layer for controlled backend tool execution.
  • Tool Registry concept for tool discovery and playbook constraints.
  • Financial Operations playbook for margin, revenue, discounts, COGS, and product mix diagnostics.
  • Executive Operations / Delivery-oriented playbook for roadmap, delivery, ITSM, PMO, meetings, tasks, and KPI anomaly analysis.
  • Synthetic enterprise dataset with connected business domains.
  • PostgreSQL-backed metric tools.
  • Qdrant/MinIO-backed document evidence direction for RAG.
  • Playbook routing based on user intent.
  • Execution timeline and run details for transparency.
  • Same-language response handling for user-facing answers.
  • Initial guardrails against wrong fallback behavior, repeated tool calls, and unsupported analysis paths.

docs.title: Synthetic enterprise dataset
docs.text:
Prepared synthetic enterprise demo data for a fashion/retail/manufacturing company, including connected business domains:

  • Financial - margin, revenue, discounts, COGS, product mix
  • Delivery - roadmap and delivery metrics
  • ITSM - service and incident context
  • PMO - project and portfolio management data
  • Meetings - decisions and meeting notes
  • Documents - document evidence for RAG
  • Scenario-based anomalies - cross-domain diagnostic scenarios

docs.title: Tool Gateway pattern
docs.text: All data access goes through controlled HTTP tools with explicit input contracts, validation, structured output, and metadata.

docs.title: Playbook-based diagnostics
docs.text:
The system routes questions to domain-specific diagnostic playbooks instead of exposing all tools to the LLM at once.

Each business domain exposes a bounded set of allowed tools, diagnostic steps, constraints, and expected evidence.

docs.title: Tool Registry
docs.text: Implemented and evolved the Tool Registry concept as a machine-readable catalog of available tools, schemas, domains, constraints, and allowed playbooks.

docs.title: Evidence-first answers
docs.text: Final responses must be based on tool outputs, document evidence, calculations, or explicitly stated limitations.

docs.title: Run trace as a trust layer
docs.text: Each diagnostic run preserves the selected playbook, tool calls, parameters, outputs, and reasoning checkpoints for debugging and audit.

docs.title: Evidence and transparency approach
docs.text: Developed the evidence and transparency approach: selected playbook, tool calls, parameters, tool results, execution timeline, run details, and JSON-level debug visibility.

docs.title: Controlled LLM execution
docs.text: The LLM reasons and plans, but data access is delegated to controlled backend tools - not free chat over corporate data.

docs.title: Controlled tool access model
docs.text: Designed the controlled tool access model where the LLM never queries PostgreSQL, Qdrant, or MinIO directly.

docs.title: Backend as control plane
docs.text: The backend defines available tools, permissions, validation rules, execution boundaries, auditability, and response structure.

docs.title: Lab runtime separated from target architecture
docs.text: LangGraph and Open WebUI are used as fast MVP/lab tools. The target product architecture assumes a backend-native control plane, dedicated UI, Tool Gateway, semantic layer, report service, and audit trail.

docs.title: Open WebUI as temporary interface
docs.text:
Configured Open WebUI as a temporary chat interface for MVP demonstration rather than building a dedicated executive UI upfront.

See also Architecture Decision Records.

docs.title: Financial Performance Diagnosis
docs.text:
Example question:

Why did gross margin drop in March?

The system selects a financial diagnostic playbook, calls metric tools for gross margin, revenue, discounts, COGS, and product mix, then produces an executive summary with evidence and limitations.

docs.title: Operational / KPI Anomaly Diagnosis
docs.text:
Example question:

Why is time-to-market unstable while local team KPIs look normal?

The system selects an operational diagnostic playbook and investigates delivery, PMO, ITSM, meeting decisions, and related evidence to detect cross-functional bottlenecks that are not visible in isolated KPI dashboards.

docs.title: Cross-Domain Management Hypothesis
docs.text:
Target scenario:

Identify the top problematic projects, explain the selection criteria, describe the issue for each project, and prepare a meeting agenda for product owners.

This scenario demonstrates the intended product direction: not just retrieving delayed tasks, but turning structured and document evidence into a management-ready diagnostic brief.

docs.title: "What can you do?"
docs.text: Available playbooks and tools

docs.title: Financial playbook: gross margin drop hypothesis
docs.text: Financial performance diagnosis with tool-based evidence Financial performance diagnosis chars report Financial performance diagnosis called tools

docs.title: Executive Operations playbook: KPI anomaly
docs.text: Operational anomaly diagnosis across delivery, ITSM, PMO, and documents

docs.title: What this demonstrates
docs.text:
This project demonstrates my ability to move from classical systems analysis into enterprise AI solution architecture.

It shows that I can take an ambiguous AI product idea and turn it into a working, constrained, and demonstrable system: define the architecture, model the data, design diagnostic workflows, build a prototype, prepare synthetic scenarios, expose controlled tools, and make LLM outputs traceable enough for enterprise discussion.

The core value is not "using an LLM". The core value is designing a system where AI reasoning is bounded by architecture, evidence, tool contracts, and auditability.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
An enterprise AI prototype for evidence-based managerial analytics.

The system is designed around a simple principle: the LLM does not “answer from memory” and does not get direct access to business data. Instead, it operates inside a controlled execution loop: it selects a diagnostic playbook, calls permitted backend tools, retrieves structured metrics and document evidence, preserves the execution trace, and generates an executive-level answer grounded in verifiable data.

The product direction is an AI Executive Analyst with verifiable evidence: a decision-support layer that helps executives and domain owners investigate business questions, detect operational risks, explain deviations, and prepare management actions without relying on opaque AI reasoning.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Typical executive analytics requires manual work across BI reports, spreadsheets, task trackers, meeting notes, documents, and domain experts. LLMs can help with synthesis, but a free-form chat over corporate data is unsafe and unreliable: it can hallucinate, lose context, call the wrong data source, or produce conclusions that cannot be audited.

This project explores how to make LLM-based managerial analytics controlled, traceable, and useful for enterprise environments.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Botanical SaaS is a multi-tenant SaaS platform for botanical gardens, nurseries, plant breeders, and private plant collections.

The product replaces fragmented spreadsheets, local databases, and outdated desktop tools with a web-based system for managing living plant collections, scientific taxonomy, geospatial data, public collection pages, QR labels, imports, lists, and controlled data sharing.

My contribution covered domain modeling, backend architecture, access control design, data model design, API structure, technical documentation, hands-on backend implementation, and cloud-ready deployment preparation.

docs.title: Product Problem
docs.text:
Botanical collections are often managed through disconnected spreadsheets, local databases, and institution-specific workflows. This makes it difficult to:

  • keep plant records consistent;
  • link plants to verified taxonomy;
  • track plant locations and lifecycle changes;
  • manage access across departments and organizations;
  • import historical data safely;
  • publish selected collection data publicly;
  • exchange information between institutions.

Botanical SaaS addresses this by providing a unified operational platform for living plant collections.

docs.title: Collection Management
docs.text:
The system supports accessioned plant records with metadata, status, provenance, growing conditions, custom fields, photos, lists, and lifecycle-related data.

It includes plant search, filtering, batch operations, soft delete, restore workflows, and Excel export.

docs.title: GIS and Mapping
docs.text:
The system treats spatial data as a core domain concern.

It supports geospatial plant and place data, PostGIS points and polygons, garden places, greenhouses, beds, map editing, plant positioning, and public map scenarios.

docs.title: My Role
docs.text:
I acted as a co-founder and technical owner of the system architecture.

My work included:

  • translating domain knowledge into a structured SaaS domain model;
  • designing the backend architecture, APIs, data structures, and service boundaries;
  • implementing backend features and integration logic;
  • designing tenant-aware access control and organization-scoped permissions;
  • modeling plant instances, taxonomy, places, lists, photos, imports, and public pages;
  • preparing architecture documentation, C4 diagrams, ADR-style decisions, and implementation context;
  • coordinating technical decisions with a botanical domain expert;
  • using AI-assisted development tools in a controlled workflow to accelerate routine implementation while keeping architecture, data model, API contracts, reviews, and deployment decisions under manual control.

docs.title: AI-assisted Development Approach
docs.text:
The project was built with an AI-assisted engineering workflow.

I used LLM-based tools to accelerate repetitive implementation tasks, generate boilerplate, and iterate faster. The core decisions remained manually controlled:

  • requirements interpretation;
  • domain model design;
  • architecture decisions;
  • API contracts;
  • database boundaries;
  • access model;
  • code review;
  • debugging;
  • deployment decisions;
  • documentation.

This workflow is directly applicable to MVP rescue work: reviewing AI-generated code, identifying structural issues, stabilizing implementation, and turning a prototype into a maintainable deployable system.

docs.title: Scientific Taxonomy
docs.text:
Plant records are tied to a centralized taxonomy layer based on POWO/IPNI reference data.

The model supports family, genus, species, cultivars, grexes, global taxa, and tenant-owned local cultivated entities.

docs.title: API Design Approach
docs.text:
The backend exposes a REST/JSON API for managing living plant collections in a multi-tenant SaaS environment. API contracts are organized around domain capabilities: plant inventory, taxonomy lookup, batch operations, import workflows, public pages, media access and exchange scenarios.

Security is enforced at several layers:

  • JWT-based authentication through Spring Security.
  • Method-level authorization for sensitive operations.
  • Tenant isolation through organization/root tenant context.
  • Data-level filtering by tenant ownership.
  • Bean Validation for request DTOs.
  • Controlled public DTOs for anonymous endpoints.
  • Soft delete for recoverable destructive actions.
  • Centralized error handling with standard HTTP statuses.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Retrieve a paginated list of plant instances available to the current user within the active organization context.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/inventory/plants

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
keyword          optional text search
organization     optional tenant/root context
unit             optional department or collection unit
list             optional list filter
recursive        optional flag for nested units
filter           optional faceted filters
page             pagination parameter
size             pagination parameter
sort             sorting parameter

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "items": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "displayName": "string",
      "taxon": "summary",
      "inventoryCode": "string",
      "status": "string",
      "location": "summary",
      "visibility": "string"
    }
  ],
  "page": {
    "number": 0,
    "size": 25,
    "totalElements": 0
  }
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Result set is restricted to the tenant context available to the current user.
  • Query specifications apply tenant filtering before returning data.
  • Filter expressions are parsed into allowlisted criteria rather than executed as raw dynamic queries.
  • Pagination and sorting use framework-level pageable parameters.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Retrieve a single plant instance as a digital record of a real plant in a living collection.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/inventory/plants/{plantId}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "id": "uuid",
  "taxon": {
    "id": "uuid",
    "displayName": "string",
    "type": "species | cultivar | grex"
  },
  "inventory": {
    "accessionNumber": "string",
    "individualCode": "string",
    "status": "string"
  },
  "organization": "summary",
  "location": "summary",
  "photos": ["summary"],
  "customFields": {}
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Access is checked against the plant’s tenant ownership and the user’s organization memberships.
  • Internal fields are not exposed through public-facing contracts.
  • Non-existing or inaccessible resources are returned through standard error responses.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Create a new plant instance in an organization’s living collection.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: POST /api/inventory/plants

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "taxonId": "uuid",
  "organizationUnitId": "uuid",
  "placeId": "uuid",
  "inventoryData": {},
  "status": "string",
  "customFields": {}
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
201 Created

{
  "id": "uuid",
  "displayName": "string",
  "taxon": "summary",
  "inventoryCode": "string",
  "status": "string"
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires administrative or editor-level access to the target organizational unit.
  • Request body is validated before domain processing.
  • taxonId is mandatory and must refer to an allowed species, cultivar or grex visible in the current tenant context.
  • Creation is delegated to the service layer after authorization and validation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Update an existing plant instance, including inventory metadata, taxon reference, status or organizational placement.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: PUT /api/inventory/plants/{plantId}

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "taxonId": "uuid",
  "organizationUnitId": "uuid",
  "placeId": "uuid",
  "inventoryData": {},
  "status": "string",
  "customFields": {}
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "id": "uuid",
  "displayName": "string",
  "taxon": "summary",
  "status": "string",
  "location": "summary"
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires write access to the existing plant record.
  • If the plant is moved to another organizational unit, access to both the current and target contexts must be validated.
  • Invalid move attempts are rejected with an access error.
  • Request validation is performed through typed request DTOs and Bean Validation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Move a plant record to trash without physically deleting it from the database.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: DELETE /api/inventory/plants/{plantId}

docs.title: Response
docs.text: 204 No Content

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires administrative access to the plant’s organizational context.
  • Deletion is recoverable by default.
  • Soft-deleted records are excluded from normal queries.
  • Permanent deletion is restricted to elevated platform-level roles.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Perform bulk actions on selected plant instances: move, update, clone, generate label data or mark labels as printed.

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text:
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/move
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/update
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/clone
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/label-data
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/mark-printed

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "plantIds": ["uuid"],
  "operationPayload": {}
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "successCount": 0,
  "failureCount": 0,
  "results": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "status": "success | failed",
      "message": "string"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Each affected plant must be checked against tenant ownership and user permissions.
  • Batch operations should not bypass per-resource authorization.
  • Partial success is supported for operations where some records may fail validation or authorization.
  • Input collections are validated before processing.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Return controlled dictionary values used by plant forms, such as lifecycle states, growing conditions or acquisition forms.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/inventory/dictionaries/{dictionaryType}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
[
  {
    "id": "string",
    "code": "string",
    "label": "string"
  }
]

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user for internal dictionaries.
  • Dictionary responses contain only safe reference data.
  • Values are controlled by the platform or tenant configuration, not arbitrary free text.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Provide a unified lookup over species, cultivars and grexes when creating or editing a plant instance.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/taxonomy/search

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
query       search term
type        optional taxon type filter
page        pagination parameter
size        pagination parameter

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "items": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "displayName": "string",
      "taxonType": "species | cultivar | grex",
      "source": "reference | global | local"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Global reference species are available across tenants.
  • Global cultivated entities are available across tenants.
  • Local cultivated entities are visible only to allowed tenant contexts.
  • The API hides internal visibility mechanics and exposes only selectable taxon summaries.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Import existing collection data from spreadsheets through a staged workflow.

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text:
POST /api/import/sessions
POST /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/sheet
POST /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/mapping
POST /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/resolve
POST /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/execute
GET  /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/status
GET  /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/error-report

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
Upload file
→ Select sheet
→ Map columns
→ Resolve values
→ Confirm uncertain matches
→ Execute import
→ Review results

docs.title: Response Example
docs.text:
{
  "sessionId": "uuid",
  "status": "uploaded | mapped | resolving | ready | executing | completed | failed",
  "progress": {
    "processedRows": 0,
    "totalRows": 0
  }
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires tenant context and import permission.
  • Uploaded files are stored outside the relational database.
  • Import sessions are tenant-scoped.
  • Ambiguous matches require user confirmation.
  • Invalid rows are reported without blocking all valid rows.
  • Background jobs are used for long-running import stages.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Expose a safe public view of a plant record, commonly used as a QR label target.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/public/plants/{publicSlug}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "displayName": "string",
  "taxon": "public summary",
  "organization": "public summary",
  "photos": ["public media reference"],
  "publicDescription": "string"
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Does not require authentication.
  • Returns only public DTOs.
  • Excludes internal identifiers, tenant metadata, private notes, internal inventory fields and restricted photos.
  • Requires public visibility settings on the owning organization and plant/list context.
  • Media access uses short-lived signed URLs or equivalent controlled delivery.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Find potential exchange matches between one organization’s wishlist and other organizations’ shared exchange lists.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/exchange/matches/{wishlistId}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "wishlist": "summary",
  "matches": [
    {
      "taxon": "summary",
      "sourceOrganization": "public or shared summary",
      "availableMaterial": "summary",
      "visibility": "string"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • User must have access to the source wishlist.
  • Candidate matches are limited to data explicitly shared for exchange or visible to the relevant community scope.
  • Matching is based on taxon identifiers rather than raw text names.
  • Private collection data is not disclosed through the exchange API.

docs.title: Error Handling Pattern
docs.text:
{
  "status": 400,
  "code": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
  "message": "Request validation failed",
  "details": []
}

Typical response statuses:

200 OK                  successful read or update
201 Created             resource created
204 No Content          successful deletion
400 Bad Request         invalid input or domain validation failure
403 Forbidden           authenticated but not allowed
404 Not Found           resource missing or not visible
409 Conflict            state conflict or resource in use
429 Too Many Requests   rate limit exceeded

docs.title: Security Summary
docs.text:
The API design combines framework-level and domain-level protection:

  • Spring Security authenticates requests and establishes user context.
  • JWT carries identity, while roles and memberships are resolved server-side.
  • Method-level checks protect write and administrative actions.
  • Tenant-scoped queries prevent cross-organization data leakage.
  • DTO validation blocks malformed requests before business logic execution.
  • Public endpoints use separate response models with restricted field sets.
  • Soft delete protects against accidental data loss.
  • Platform-level operations are separated from tenant-level workflows.
  • Audit and login events provide traceability for sensitive actions.

docs.title: Smart Import
docs.text:
The platform includes a guided spreadsheet import pipeline for existing plant collections.

The import flow supports file upload, sheet selection, column mapping, value resolution, fuzzy matching, asynchronous processing, row-level results, and error report export.

docs.title: Multi-tenancy and Access Control
docs.text:
The platform uses a soft multi-tenancy model based on a root organization unit pattern.

Users can belong to multiple organizations and have different roles depending on the current organization context. Access checks are enforced across service, repository, API, and UI layers.

docs.title: Public Showcase
docs.text:
Organizations can expose selected data through public pages, public plant pages, public lists, QR-label targets, and a global map.

The public layer uses separate DTOs and visibility rules to avoid leaking internal fields.

docs.title: Infrastructure and Deployment
docs.text:
The project is designed as a practical cloud-ready MVP:

  • separated frontend and backend;
  • containerized services;
  • PostgreSQL/PostGIS database;
  • MinIO/S3-compatible media and import storage;
  • Flyway-controlled schema migrations;
  • production profile based on environment configuration;
  • basic CI/CD and deployment automation path.

docs.title: Decoupled Frontend and Backend
docs.text: The system uses a separated Angular frontend and Spring Boot backend API. This keeps UI evolution independent from backend domain logic and supports future client channels.

docs.title: Root-unit Soft Multi-tenancy
docs.text: Each tenant is represented by a root organization unit. Tenant-scoped entities carry a root_unit_id, and access is constrained through repositories, specifications, services, and API-level checks.

docs.title: Context-aware Authorization
docs.text:
Permissions depend not only on global user roles, but also on the currently selected organization and unit context.

This fits B2B scenarios where the same user may have different permissions in different organizations or departments.

docs.title: PostGIS as a Core Domain Layer
docs.text: Plant locations, garden places, greenhouses, beds, and polygons are modeled as spatial data, not as decorative map overlays.

docs.title: Hybrid Current State and History Model
docs.text: The system separates current operational state from history/audit-related data. This keeps day-to-day queries efficient while preserving traceability of important changes.

docs.title: Controlled Public Exposure
docs.text: Public pages, plant cards, lists, photos, and map data are exposed through dedicated public endpoints and DTOs. Visibility rules prevent accidental exposure of internal tenant data.

docs.title: Root-unit soft multi-tenancy vs database-per-tenant
docs.text:
This keeps early-stage operational complexity lower than database-per-tenant while preserving isolation and shared reference data.

See also Architecture Decision Records.

docs.title: What this demonstrates
docs.text:
This project demonstrates my ability to work across system analysis, backend design, implementation, and deployment preparation.

It is especially relevant to:

  • SaaS MVP rescue and stabilization;
  • backend/API restructuring;
  • multi-tenant business applications;
  • database and migration cleanup;
  • GIS-enabled systems;
  • spreadsheet import pipelines;
  • cloud deployment preparation;
  • architecture documentation for fast-moving teams;
  • controlled use of AI-assisted development.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Botanical SaaS is a multi-tenant SaaS platform for botanical gardens, nurseries, plant breeders, and private plant collections.

The product replaces fragmented spreadsheets, local databases, and outdated desktop tools with a web-based system for managing living plant collections, scientific taxonomy, geospatial data, public collection pages, QR labels, imports, lists, and controlled data sharing.

My contribution covered domain modeling, backend architecture, access control design, data model design, API structure, technical documentation, hands-on backend implementation, and cloud-ready deployment preparation.

docs.title: Product Problem
docs.text:
Botanical collections are often managed through disconnected spreadsheets, local databases, and institution-specific workflows. This makes it difficult to:

  • keep plant records consistent;
  • link plants to verified taxonomy;
  • track plant locations and lifecycle changes;
  • manage access across departments and organizations;
  • import historical data safely;
  • publish selected collection data publicly;
  • exchange information between institutions.

Botanical SaaS addresses this by providing a unified operational platform for living plant collections.

docs.title: Collection Management
docs.text:
The system supports accessioned plant records with metadata, status, provenance, growing conditions, custom fields, photos, lists, and lifecycle-related data.

It includes plant search, filtering, batch operations, soft delete, restore workflows, and Excel export.

docs.title: GIS and Mapping
docs.text:
The system treats spatial data as a core domain concern.

It supports geospatial plant and place data, PostGIS points and polygons, garden places, greenhouses, beds, map editing, plant positioning, and public map scenarios.

docs.title: My Role
docs.text:
I acted as a co-founder and technical owner of the system architecture.

My work included:

  • translating domain knowledge into a structured SaaS domain model;
  • designing the backend architecture, APIs, data structures, and service boundaries;
  • implementing backend features and integration logic;
  • designing tenant-aware access control and organization-scoped permissions;
  • modeling plant instances, taxonomy, places, lists, photos, imports, and public pages;
  • preparing architecture documentation, C4 diagrams, ADR-style decisions, and implementation context;
  • coordinating technical decisions with a botanical domain expert;
  • using AI-assisted development tools in a controlled workflow to accelerate routine implementation while keeping architecture, data model, API contracts, reviews, and deployment decisions under manual control.

docs.title: AI-assisted Development Approach
docs.text:
The project was built with an AI-assisted engineering workflow.

I used LLM-based tools to accelerate repetitive implementation tasks, generate boilerplate, and iterate faster. The core decisions remained manually controlled:

  • requirements interpretation;
  • domain model design;
  • architecture decisions;
  • API contracts;
  • database boundaries;
  • access model;
  • code review;
  • debugging;
  • deployment decisions;
  • documentation.

This workflow is directly applicable to MVP rescue work: reviewing AI-generated code, identifying structural issues, stabilizing implementation, and turning a prototype into a maintainable deployable system.

docs.title: Scientific Taxonomy
docs.text:
Plant records are tied to a centralized taxonomy layer based on POWO/IPNI reference data.

The model supports family, genus, species, cultivars, grexes, global taxa, and tenant-owned local cultivated entities.

docs.title: API Design Approach
docs.text:
The backend exposes a REST/JSON API for managing living plant collections in a multi-tenant SaaS environment. API contracts are organized around domain capabilities: plant inventory, taxonomy lookup, batch operations, import workflows, public pages, media access and exchange scenarios.

Security is enforced at several layers:

  • JWT-based authentication through Spring Security.
  • Method-level authorization for sensitive operations.
  • Tenant isolation through organization/root tenant context.
  • Data-level filtering by tenant ownership.
  • Bean Validation for request DTOs.
  • Controlled public DTOs for anonymous endpoints.
  • Soft delete for recoverable destructive actions.
  • Centralized error handling with standard HTTP statuses.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Retrieve a paginated list of plant instances available to the current user within the active organization context.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/inventory/plants

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
keyword          optional text search
organization     optional tenant/root context
unit             optional department or collection unit
list             optional list filter
recursive        optional flag for nested units
filter           optional faceted filters
page             pagination parameter
size             pagination parameter
sort             sorting parameter

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "items": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "displayName": "string",
      "taxon": "summary",
      "inventoryCode": "string",
      "status": "string",
      "location": "summary",
      "visibility": "string"
    }
  ],
  "page": {
    "number": 0,
    "size": 25,
    "totalElements": 0
  }
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Result set is restricted to the tenant context available to the current user.
  • Query specifications apply tenant filtering before returning data.
  • Filter expressions are parsed into allowlisted criteria rather than executed as raw dynamic queries.
  • Pagination and sorting use framework-level pageable parameters.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Retrieve a single plant instance as a digital record of a real plant in a living collection.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/inventory/plants/{plantId}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "id": "uuid",
  "taxon": {
    "id": "uuid",
    "displayName": "string",
    "type": "species | cultivar | grex"
  },
  "inventory": {
    "accessionNumber": "string",
    "individualCode": "string",
    "status": "string"
  },
  "organization": "summary",
  "location": "summary",
  "photos": ["summary"],
  "customFields": {}
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Access is checked against the plant’s tenant ownership and the user’s organization memberships.
  • Internal fields are not exposed through public-facing contracts.
  • Non-existing or inaccessible resources are returned through standard error responses.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Create a new plant instance in an organization’s living collection.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: POST /api/inventory/plants

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "taxonId": "uuid",
  "organizationUnitId": "uuid",
  "placeId": "uuid",
  "inventoryData": {},
  "status": "string",
  "customFields": {}
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
201 Created

{
  "id": "uuid",
  "displayName": "string",
  "taxon": "summary",
  "inventoryCode": "string",
  "status": "string"
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires administrative or editor-level access to the target organizational unit.
  • Request body is validated before domain processing.
  • taxonId is mandatory and must refer to an allowed species, cultivar or grex visible in the current tenant context.
  • Creation is delegated to the service layer after authorization and validation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Update an existing plant instance, including inventory metadata, taxon reference, status or organizational placement.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: PUT /api/inventory/plants/{plantId}

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "taxonId": "uuid",
  "organizationUnitId": "uuid",
  "placeId": "uuid",
  "inventoryData": {},
  "status": "string",
  "customFields": {}
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "id": "uuid",
  "displayName": "string",
  "taxon": "summary",
  "status": "string",
  "location": "summary"
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires write access to the existing plant record.
  • If the plant is moved to another organizational unit, access to both the current and target contexts must be validated.
  • Invalid move attempts are rejected with an access error.
  • Request validation is performed through typed request DTOs and Bean Validation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Move a plant record to trash without physically deleting it from the database.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: DELETE /api/inventory/plants/{plantId}

docs.title: Response
docs.text: 204 No Content

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires administrative access to the plant’s organizational context.
  • Deletion is recoverable by default.
  • Soft-deleted records are excluded from normal queries.
  • Permanent deletion is restricted to elevated platform-level roles.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Perform bulk actions on selected plant instances: move, update, clone, generate label data or mark labels as printed.

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text:
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/move
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/update
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/clone
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/label-data
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/mark-printed

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "plantIds": ["uuid"],
  "operationPayload": {}
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "successCount": 0,
  "failureCount": 0,
  "results": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "status": "success | failed",
      "message": "string"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Each affected plant must be checked against tenant ownership and user permissions.
  • Batch operations should not bypass per-resource authorization.
  • Partial success is supported for operations where some records may fail validation or authorization.
  • Input collections are validated before processing.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Return controlled dictionary values used by plant forms, such as lifecycle states, growing conditions or acquisition forms.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/inventory/dictionaries/{dictionaryType}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
[
  {
    "id": "string",
    "code": "string",
    "label": "string"
  }
]

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user for internal dictionaries.
  • Dictionary responses contain only safe reference data.
  • Values are controlled by the platform or tenant configuration, not arbitrary free text.

docs.text:
The platform includes a guided spreadsheet import pipeline for existing plant collections.

The import flow supports file upload, sheet selection, column mapping, value resolution, fuzzy matching, asynchronous processing, row-level results, and error report export.

docs.title: Multi-tenancy and Access Control
docs.text:
The platform uses a soft multi-tenancy model based on a root organization unit pattern.

Users can belong to multiple organizations and have different roles depending on the current organization context. Access checks are enforced across service, repository, API, and UI layers.

docs.title: Public Showcase
docs.text:
Organizations can expose selected data through public pages, public plant pages, public lists, QR-label targets, and a global map.

The public layer uses separate DTOs and visibility rules to avoid leaking internal fields.

docs.title: Infrastructure and Deployment
docs.text:
The project is designed as a practical cloud-ready MVP:

  • separated frontend and backend;
  • containerized services;
  • PostgreSQL/PostGIS database;
  • MinIO/S3-compatible media and import storage;
  • Flyway-controlled schema migrations;
  • production profile based on environment configuration;
  • basic CI/CD and deployment automation path.

docs.title: Decoupled Frontend and Backend
docs.text: The system uses a separated Angular frontend and Spring Boot backend API. This keeps UI evolution independent from backend domain logic and supports future client channels.

docs.title: Root-unit Soft Multi-tenancy
docs.text: Each tenant is represented by a root organization unit. Tenant-scoped entities carry a root_unit_id, and access is constrained through repositories, specifications, services, and API-level checks.

docs.title: Context-aware Authorization
docs.text:
Permissions depend not only on global user roles, but also on the currently selected organization and unit context.

This fits B2B scenarios where the same user may have different permissions in different organizations or departments.

docs.title: PostGIS as a Core Domain Layer
docs.text: Plant locations, garden places, greenhouses, beds, and polygons are modeled as spatial data, not as decorative map overlays.

docs.title: Hybrid Current State and History Model
docs.text: The system separates current operational state from history/audit-related data. This keeps day-to-day queries efficient while preserving traceability of important changes.

docs.title: Controlled Public Exposure
docs.text: Public pages, plant cards, lists, photos, and map data are exposed through dedicated public endpoints and DTOs. Visibility rules prevent accidental exposure of internal tenant data.

docs.title: Root-unit soft multi-tenancy vs database-per-tenant
docs.text:
This keeps early-stage operational complexity lower than database-per-tenant while preserving isolation and shared reference data.

See also Architecture Decision Records.

It is especially relevant to:

docs.title: Collection Management
docs.text:
The system supports accessioned plant records with metadata, status, provenance, growing conditions, custom fields, photos, lists, and lifecycle-related data.

It includes plant search, filtering, batch operations, soft delete, restore workflows, and Excel export.

docs.title: GIS and Mapping
docs.text:
The system treats spatial data as a core domain concern.

It supports geospatial plant and place data, PostGIS points and polygons, garden places, greenhouses, beds, map editing, plant positioning, and public map scenarios.

docs.title: Scientific Taxonomy
docs.text:
Plant records are tied to a centralized taxonomy layer based on POWO/IPNI reference data.

The model supports family, genus, species, cultivars, grexes, global taxa, and tenant-owned local cultivated entities.

docs.title: API Design Approach
docs.text:
The backend exposes a REST/JSON API for managing living plant collections in a multi-tenant SaaS environment. API contracts are organized around domain capabilities: plant inventory, taxonomy lookup, batch operations, import workflows, public pages, media access and exchange scenarios.

Security is enforced at several layers:

  • JWT-based authentication through Spring Security.
  • Method-level authorization for sensitive operations.
  • Tenant isolation through organization/root tenant context.
  • Data-level filtering by tenant ownership.
  • Bean Validation for request DTOs.
  • Controlled public DTOs for anonymous endpoints.
  • Soft delete for recoverable destructive actions.
  • Centralized error handling with standard HTTP statuses.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Retrieve a paginated list of plant instances available to the current user within the active organization context.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/inventory/plants

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
keyword          optional text search
organization     optional tenant/root context
unit             optional department or collection unit
list             optional list filter
recursive        optional flag for nested units
filter           optional faceted filters
page             pagination parameter
size             pagination parameter
sort             sorting parameter

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "items": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "displayName": "string",
      "taxon": "summary",
      "inventoryCode": "string",
      "status": "string",
      "location": "summary",
      "visibility": "string"
    }
  ],
  "page": {
    "number": 0,
    "size": 25,
    "totalElements": 0
  }
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Result set is restricted to the tenant context available to the current user.
  • Query specifications apply tenant filtering before returning data.
  • Filter expressions are parsed into allowlisted criteria rather than executed as raw dynamic queries.
  • Pagination and sorting use framework-level pageable parameters.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Retrieve a single plant instance as a digital record of a real plant in a living collection.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/inventory/plants/{plantId}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "id": "uuid",
  "taxon": {
    "id": "uuid",
    "displayName": "string",
    "type": "species | cultivar | grex"
  },
  "inventory": {
    "accessionNumber": "string",
    "individualCode": "string",
    "status": "string"
  },
  "organization": "summary",
  "location": "summary",
  "photos": ["summary"],
  "customFields": {}
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Access is checked against the plant’s tenant ownership and the user’s organization memberships.
  • Internal fields are not exposed through public-facing contracts.
  • Non-existing or inaccessible resources are returned through standard error responses.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Create a new plant instance in an organization’s living collection.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: POST /api/inventory/plants

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "taxonId": "uuid",
  "organizationUnitId": "uuid",
  "placeId": "uuid",
  "inventoryData": {},
  "status": "string",
  "customFields": {}
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
201 Created

{
  "id": "uuid",
  "displayName": "string",
  "taxon": "summary",
  "inventoryCode": "string",
  "status": "string"
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires administrative or editor-level access to the target organizational unit.
  • Request body is validated before domain processing.
  • taxonId is mandatory and must refer to an allowed species, cultivar or grex visible in the current tenant context.
  • Creation is delegated to the service layer after authorization and validation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Update an existing plant instance, including inventory metadata, taxon reference, status or organizational placement.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: PUT /api/inventory/plants/{plantId}

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "taxonId": "uuid",
  "organizationUnitId": "uuid",
  "placeId": "uuid",
  "inventoryData": {},
  "status": "string",
  "customFields": {}
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "id": "uuid",
  "displayName": "string",
  "taxon": "summary",
  "status": "string",
  "location": "summary"
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires write access to the existing plant record.
  • If the plant is moved to another organizational unit, access to both the current and target contexts must be validated.
  • Invalid move attempts are rejected with an access error.
  • Request validation is performed through typed request DTOs and Bean Validation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Move a plant record to trash without physically deleting it from the database.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: DELETE /api/inventory/plants/{plantId}

docs.title: Response
docs.text: 204 No Content

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires administrative access to the plant’s organizational context.
  • Deletion is recoverable by default.
  • Soft-deleted records are excluded from normal queries.
  • Permanent deletion is restricted to elevated platform-level roles.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Perform bulk actions on selected plant instances: move, update, clone, generate label data or mark labels as printed.

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text:
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/move
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/update
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/clone
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/label-data
POST /api/inventory/plants/batch/mark-printed

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "plantIds": ["uuid"],
  "operationPayload": {}
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "successCount": 0,
  "failureCount": 0,
  "results": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "status": "success | failed",
      "message": "string"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Each affected plant must be checked against tenant ownership and user permissions.
  • Batch operations should not bypass per-resource authorization.
  • Partial success is supported for operations where some records may fail validation or authorization.
  • Input collections are validated before processing.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Return controlled dictionary values used by plant forms, such as lifecycle states, growing conditions or acquisition forms.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/inventory/dictionaries/{dictionaryType}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
[
  {
    "id": "string",
    "code": "string",
    "label": "string"
  }
]

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user for internal dictionaries.
  • Dictionary responses contain only safe reference data.
  • Values are controlled by the platform or tenant configuration, not arbitrary free text.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Provide a unified lookup over species, cultivars and grexes when creating or editing a plant instance.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/taxonomy/search

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
query       search term
type        optional taxon type filter
page        pagination parameter
size        pagination parameter

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "items": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "displayName": "string",
      "taxonType": "species | cultivar | grex",
      "source": "reference | global | local"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Global reference species are available across tenants.
  • Global cultivated entities are available across tenants.
  • Local cultivated entities are visible only to allowed tenant contexts.
  • The API hides internal visibility mechanics and exposes only selectable taxon summaries.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Import existing collection data from spreadsheets through a staged workflow.

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text:
POST /api/import/sessions
POST /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/sheet
POST /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/mapping
POST /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/resolve
POST /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/execute
GET  /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/status
GET  /api/import/sessions/{sessionId}/error-report

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
Upload file
→ Select sheet
→ Map columns
→ Resolve values
→ Confirm uncertain matches
→ Execute import
→ Review results

docs.title: Response Example
docs.text:
{
  "sessionId": "uuid",
  "status": "uploaded | mapped | resolving | ready | executing | completed | failed",
  "progress": {
    "processedRows": 0,
    "totalRows": 0
  }
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • Requires tenant context and import permission.
  • Uploaded files are stored outside the relational database.
  • Import sessions are tenant-scoped.
  • Ambiguous matches require user confirmation.
  • Invalid rows are reported without blocking all valid rows.
  • Background jobs are used for long-running import stages.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Expose a safe public view of a plant record, commonly used as a QR label target.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/public/plants/{publicSlug}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "displayName": "string",
  "taxon": "public summary",
  "organization": "public summary",
  "photos": ["public media reference"],
  "publicDescription": "string"
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Does not require authentication.
  • Returns only public DTOs.
  • Excludes internal identifiers, tenant metadata, private notes, internal inventory fields and restricted photos.
  • Requires public visibility settings on the owning organization and plant/list context.
  • Media access uses short-lived signed URLs or equivalent controlled delivery.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Find potential exchange matches between one organization’s wishlist and other organizations’ shared exchange lists.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text: GET /api/exchange/matches/{wishlistId}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "wishlist": "summary",
  "matches": [
    {
      "taxon": "summary",
      "sourceOrganization": "public or shared summary",
      "availableMaterial": "summary",
      "visibility": "string"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Requires authenticated user.
  • User must have access to the source wishlist.
  • Candidate matches are limited to data explicitly shared for exchange or visible to the relevant community scope.
  • Matching is based on taxon identifiers rather than raw text names.
  • Private collection data is not disclosed through the exchange API.

docs.title: Error Handling Pattern
docs.text:
{
  "status": 400,
  "code": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
  "message": "Request validation failed",
  "details": []
}

Typical response statuses:

200 OK                  successful read or update
201 Created             resource created
204 No Content          successful deletion
400 Bad Request         invalid input or domain validation failure
403 Forbidden           authenticated but not allowed
404 Not Found           resource missing or not visible
409 Conflict            state conflict or resource in use
429 Too Many Requests   rate limit exceeded

docs.title: Security Summary
docs.text:
The API design combines framework-level and domain-level protection:

  • Spring Security authenticates requests and establishes user context.
  • JWT carries identity, while roles and memberships are resolved server-side.
  • Method-level checks protect write and administrative actions.
  • Tenant-scoped queries prevent cross-organization data leakage.
  • DTO validation blocks malformed requests before business logic execution.
  • Public endpoints use separate response models with restricted field sets.
  • Soft delete protects against accidental data loss.
  • Platform-level operations are separated from tenant-level workflows.
  • Audit and login events provide traceability for sensitive actions.

docs.title: What this demonstrates
docs.text:
This project demonstrates my ability to work across system analysis, backend design, implementation, and deployment preparation.

It is especially relevant to:

  • SaaS MVP rescue and stabilization;
  • backend/API restructuring;
  • multi-tenant business applications;
  • database and migration cleanup;
  • GIS-enabled systems;
  • spreadsheet import pipelines;
  • cloud deployment preparation;
  • architecture documentation for fast-moving teams;
  • controlled use of AI-assisted development.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Botanical SaaS is a multi-tenant SaaS platform for botanical gardens, nurseries, plant breeders, and private plant collections.

The product replaces fragmented spreadsheets, local databases, and outdated desktop tools with a web-based system for managing living plant collections, scientific taxonomy, geospatial data, public collection pages, QR labels, imports, lists, and controlled data sharing.

My contribution covered domain modeling, backend architecture, access control design, data model design, API structure, technical documentation, hands-on backend implementation, and cloud-ready deployment preparation.

docs.title: My Role
docs.text:
I acted as a co-founder and technical owner of the system architecture.

My work included:

  • translating domain knowledge into a structured SaaS domain model;
  • designing the backend architecture, APIs, data structures, and service boundaries;
  • implementing backend features and integration logic;
  • designing tenant-aware access control and organization-scoped permissions;
  • modeling plant instances, taxonomy, places, lists, photos, imports, and public pages;
  • preparing architecture documentation, C4 diagrams, ADR-style decisions, and implementation context;
  • coordinating technical decisions with a botanical domain expert;
  • using AI-assisted development tools in a controlled workflow to accelerate routine implementation while keeping architecture, data model, API contracts, reviews, and deployment decisions under manual control.

docs.title: AI-assisted Development Approach
docs.text:
The project was built with an AI-assisted engineering workflow.

I used LLM-based tools to accelerate repetitive implementation tasks, generate boilerplate, and iterate faster. The core decisions remained manually controlled:

  • requirements interpretation;
  • domain model design;
  • architecture decisions;
  • API contracts;
  • database boundaries;
  • access model;
  • code review;
  • debugging;
  • deployment decisions;
  • documentation.

This workflow is directly applicable to MVP rescue work: reviewing AI-generated code, identifying structural issues, stabilizing implementation, and turning a prototype into a maintainable deployable system.

docs.title: Botanical SaaS — SRS pack
docs.text: This page assembles sections relevant to a software requirements specification: context and problem through security, quality, and operations.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• Summary
